coredata.dc:title: Understanding the theranostic potential of quantum dots in cancer management
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Mohammad Sameer
Sheikh, Afsana
Abourehab, Mohammed A.S.
Gupta, Neelima
Kesharwani, Prashant
coredata.dc:description: Cancer is not left with the space untouched, however, a complete cure has not been achieved. Researchers are working extensively to bring a new edge to the treatment regimen. Unfortunately, due to dynamic genetic changes of cancerous cells and the necessity to eventually conquer malignant cell or tumor resistance, these innovations are frequently just temporary. This creates a never-ending need for overcoming resistance and improving survival. It is worth mentioning that Quantum Dots (QDs) in the field of nanotechnology has gained enormous attention in cancer diagnosis and its treatment. Owing to extensive biocompatibility, adjustable surface functions, and high-water solubility, quantum dots have become nanoparticle of interest in medical applications. The present review focuses on the various applications of quantum in the diagnosis of cancer, including early detection of primary as well as the drug delivery mediated by quantum dots. As a means of improving their functionality, we highlight various QD kinds and characterization techniques in this review. We have also concentrated on using QDs as a drug delivery system, diagnostic tool, drug delivery system, or tool to research biological processes. We conclude by talking about QDs' current limits and upcoming difficulties.

Drug delivery with the help of quantum dot have been made successful due to extraordinary properties of quantum dot. Quantum dots are being encapsulated with different semiconductor element and are then further loaded with anticancer agents which escort the drug into the circulation with prolong release.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer is not left with the space untouched, however, a complete cure has not been achieved. Researchers are working extensively to bring a new edge to the treatment regimen. Unfortunately, due to dynamic genetic changes of cancerous cells and the necessity to eventually conquer malignant cell or tumor resistance, these innovations are frequently just temporary. This creates a never-ending need for overcoming resistance and improving survival. It is worth mentioning that Quantum Dots (QDs) in the field of nanotechnology has gained enormous attention in cancer diagnosis and its treatment. Owing to extensive biocompatibility, adjustable surface functions, and high-water solubility, quantum dots have become nanoparticle of interest in medical applications. The present review focuses on the various applications of quantum in the diagnosis of cancer, including early detection of primary as well as the drug delivery mediated by quantum dots. As a means of improving their functionality, we highlight various QD kinds and characterization techniques in this review. We have also concentrated on using QDs as a drug delivery system, diagnostic tool, drug delivery system, or tool to research biological processes. We conclude by talking about QDs' current limits and upcoming difficulties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In developed and developing nations, one of the deadliest diseases is cancer and experts are always looking for better therapies. Although the therapeutic choices for cancer have grown significantly over the recent years, we are still far distant from discovering a cure-all. The delivery of traditional cancer care globally is impacted by the rise in cancer incidence and mortality. Integrative oncology offers a platform for investigating and putting mainstream cancer treatment into practise. By providing patient-centred, evidence-informed treatment, it can help close healthcare gaps. However, because of the death and treatment repercussions, is considered to be a serious global health issue. Conventional imaging, diagnosis, and treatment are still used to combat cancer. Results for patients may be impacted by the dangers, adverse effects, and limitations of these techniques. As a result, effective treatments, and diagnoses of cancer are required. There are numerous treatment options for cancer. Radiation therapy, chemotherapy, palliative care, hormone-based therapy, immunotherapy, surgery and viral-mediated therapy is a few examples, but not all of them has remarkably improved the therapeutic results. Nonetheless, nanotechnology seems to be a dependable strategy for addressing the majority of issues connected to anticancer drug delivery [1–7].

Nanomaterials provide significant promise for applications that target cancer. One of them is the class of quantum dots. The term quantum dot was suggested by Mark Reed in 1988, where ‘Quantum’ indicates a diminutive and discrete unit of any physical property, the name originates from the fact that the nanocrystals that are ‘dots’ are ordained from quantum mechanics [8–13]. QDs are also known as semiconductors or nanocrystals with tunable microscopic and targeting properties with size ranging from 100 to 200 nm. With the advancement in nanotechnology, novel QDS such as metal chalcogenide QDs have been studied where they focussed their biomedical application with special emphasis on detection and therapy of SARS-COV-2 [14,15]. The primary objective is to create small probes that can penetrate cells and organelles and have great selectivity, adaptability, and stability. They are energy-releasing aquatic nanostructures [16]. Depending on the quantum dot width, which impacts luminosity in the UV-near IR range, smaller and bigger nanocrystals emit blue [2 nm] luminescence and red luminescence (5 nm). Its combination of electrical and optical properties, sustained luminescence, and greatly reduced photo stability set it apart from other organic dyes. Under in vivo conditions the signs of toxicity are relatively not obvious due to short experimental periods and due to advanced polymer coating, which protects the integrity of QDs. Furthermore, on the basis of surface coating of different polymers such as metals, semiconductors, metalloids etc. QDs are classified as carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots, polymer dots etc. [17]. QDs also has the ability to detect cancer cells. For in vivo tumour vascular targeting in mice, a quantum dot—peptide combination is employed. In CdSe quantum dots, toxic cadmium is typically surrounded by a ZnS shell to reduce cytotoxicity, A recent study performed by Ye et.al. to evaluate the toxicity of CdSe QDs demonstrated that the initial dose of cadmium remained in the liver, spleen and kidneys even after 90 days, suggesting that the renal clearance of quantum dot is quite slow. The results demonstrated that the release of cadmium or heavy metals in blood lead to increased toxicity. Further studies are still needed to understand the ultimate fate of heavy metal combination with QDs [18, 17]. The heavy metals increased the concentration of nanoparticles presents in the vascular region that was being treated. Here the quantum dots also functioned well as control and monitoring systems. By successfully attaching to the aberrant cells, nucleoli and modifies the surface of quantum dots with tumour peptides, for instance, increases cell viability. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that combining RNA interference and quantum dots enhances gene suppression. These are also employed as quenchers of energy transport system in charge transfer mechanism, transfer system in fluorescence resonance, acceptors of energy of chemiluminescence-resonance and other applications [19].

Biocompatible quantum dots are employed in clinical system to help increase the aqueous solubility of drugs. For instance, zinc oxide, graphene, and carbon quantum dots. When delivering the anti-cancer drug such as mitomycin, carbon quantum dots are favoured. Quantum dots composed of semiconductors, such as ZnS/ZN CuInS and CdTe are frequently employed for imaging and sensing applications. For in-vitro cell labelling and in-vivo tumour cell imaging, QDs coated with organic acid are employed [20]. Quantum dots have stiff structure that offers a wide surface area for the process of drug conjugation. The two main functions used for drug binding are free amine group and carboxylic acid group. In a glass matrix and in colloidal solution, Brus and Ekimov firstly discovered them in 1980. Whereas in 1983, a new publication of the first colloidal cadmium sulphide quantum dot was discovered. The classification of quantum dot on the basis of their advantages, disadvantages, limitation and limitations has been demonstrated in Table 1.

Recent studies have focused a lot of attention on QDs because of their intriguing characteristics and wide range of applications in sensors, diagnostics, and drug therapy. Some of the most innovative methods used in the field of quantum dots are listed below.

A novel family of carbon nanomaterials with diameters below 10 nm is called carbon-based quantum dots, which includes carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots. They were first produced in year 2004 during the sample preparation electrophoresis purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes, and later in 2006 via the laser ablation of graphite and cement powder. Due to their plentiful, and affordable nature, carbon-based quantum dots with intriguing features have steadily emerged as a new nanocarbon component. Although it is often a dark substance, carbon was once thought to have poor fluorescence and a low water solubility. Due to their intense luminescence and superior solubility, carbon-based quantum dots—which are also known as carbon nanotubes—have received a lot of interest [21]. Recently, Sun and colleagues performed a research and discovered that carbon dots or carbon based quantum dots, that are nanoscale carbon particles(30–50 nm in diameter)having surface passivation to exhibit robust photoluminescence in both solid state and solution form [22]. The accessible carbon dots emission spectrum characteristics are similar to those of silicon nanocrystals that have had their surfaces oxidised. The pure carbon nanoparticles could be developed by graphite object laser ablation in an inert atmosphere at low pressure. These carbon nanoparticles appear to be mostly amorphous, according to the findings of X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The carbon nanoparticles are not emissive on their own, whether they are in suspensions or the solid form. The particles could however dissolve in common organic solvents and/or water following simple surface passivation by adding organic molecules, depending upon the attached functions. These surface-modified soluble carbon nanoparticles were well disseminated as individual particles with sizes of a few nanometres, according to the microscopic data [22].

Preliminary, in vivo investigations in mice have shown that carbon quantum dots are nontoxic and biocompatible. These have also been combined with bioactive and biological species, such as antibody and specific targeting receptors, for optical bioimaging of cancerous tissues and cells [23]. The use of carbon dots in the early identification and treatment of breast tumours is another possible bio application, for which various degrees of research are still under process. It has been demonstrated that antibodies functionalized on carbon nanotubes can successfully bind to breast cancer cells. Targeting receptor-mediated endocytosis might be another significant approach while certain peptide receptors can facilitate the internalisation of quantum dots. Intracellular accumulation of carbon dots can potentially be increased by taking advantage of the fact that cancerous cells contain more folate receptors, which are essential for cells that proliferate swiftly. Quantum dots and those contained in liposomes have both recently been shown in the studies to be delivered by the folate-receptor in this manner [24]. The combinatorial aspects of quantum dots with semiconductors for diagnosis of cancer has been demonstrated in Table 2.

Recently, 2-D graphene has been discovered as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which exhibit outstanding chemical, physical, and biological properties and a size of lesser than 100 nm. Single layer of carbon atoms develops an immaculate GQD. The size of Graphene based QDs can be modified to produce high stability properties by permitting for an increase in surface area, greater flexibility and solubility in conjugating with other nanoparticles. Due to their smaller size than graphene or graphene oxide [25], GQDs have a higher potential of being used in biological applications (GO). In investigations, GQDs showed great biocompatibility and low biotoxicity. Using lung cancer A549 cells, Xie et al. examined the cytotoxicity and activation of autophagy. The most dangerous GQDs were hGQDs; aGQDs and cGQDs had no toxicity. Therefore, for use in bio imaging, Nafiujjaman et al. created 105 nm-diameter peptide-influenced GQD/iron oxide nanoparticles. They found that GQDs still have the functional and spectroscopic properties necessary for fluorescence bioimaging. While for cancer cell targeting, the iRGD moiety was assisted which lead to MRI imaging by iron oxide nanoparticles [26].

Graphene based quantum dots were developed by Qi et al. to precisely eradicate the DNA of cancer cells in order to limit the growth of tumours. The Graphene based quantum dot (GQD) functionalized by amine were then grafted with folic acid (FA) modified PEG that targets cancer cells via disulfide bonding, further TAT peptides (TAT-NGs) were added (FAPEG-TNGs) after nucleus being targeted. The resultant FAPEG-TNGs showed tremendous nucleus uptake, biocompatibility and cancer cell targeting [17].

Studies demonstrate enough results on synthesis of water-soluble ZnS nanoparticles. These nanomaterials can interact with monoclonal antibodies, folic acid, amino acid or aptamers for more specific targeting, similar to “regular” quantum dots [26]. Precursors that have been dissolved in aqueous solution are used to create ZnS nanoparticles using the precipitation process. The end product is a suspension of colloidal nanoparticles. A terminal group, such as methacrylic acid, cysteine or chitosan appended during the synthesis to reduce agglomeration. A terminal group such as cysteine, chitosan and methacrylic acid is appended during the synthesis to reduce agglomeration. Because of the steric barrier provided by the polymer molecules adsorbed on the nanoparticles’ surfaces, osmotic repulsion forces the particles apart from one another. Fluorescent nanoparticles are created by doping Zinc Sulphide nanoparticles with Eu2+ or Mn2+. Additionally, Mn2+ or Eu2+ doped quantum dots may have significant magnetism and luminescence that might be exploited for both magnetic imaging and fluorescence. Gamma radiation is in use sometimes to promote the colloid production of zinc sulphide nanoparticles [27].

Novel bright probes have recently been found are more biocompatible than ordinary quantum dots. QDs have a hydrophobic surface as they are formed. The surface of quantum dots is altered with terminal polar groups or compounds to make them water soluble [27]. It has been proposed for biological imaging that quantum dot can be encapsulated in phospholipid micelles for biological imaging. Such nanoparticles’ lipid structures can be easily used to bioconjugate a variety of targeted compounds to the functional groups (SH, CCOH, NH2, maleimide, biotin,) present in phospholipids. In current experiment, ‘lipodots,’ or quantum dots contained in phospholipid vesicles, were made. This folate- are targeted lipodots which were used to target colon cancer cells that did not express folate receptors, but did have enhanced folate receptor expression in lymphoma cells. In order to study the quantum dots adsorption on the surface of unilamellar vesicles, fluorimetry titration and zeta potential were used, producing stabilized hybrid giant vesicles. There have been reports of brand-new silica nanoparticles flodots that have been dyed with luminous organic and inorganic compounds and dispersed throughout a silica matrix. Another illustration of a potential clinical application for nanocrystals is in nano transducers, which convert two or more incident photons into a higher energy photon. Treatment at deeper sites will be possible with these upconverting nanoparticles (UCNs), which are made of NaYF4 encapsulated with Yb3+ and Er3+ in a polymeric covering [28].

It has been proposed to design QDs using bioluminescence energy transfer (BRET). It uses QDs tagged with halo tag-luciferase or fluorescent protein luciferase as an energy source. This idea addresses the problem of light penetration. It still has certain issues, though, such as the delivery of foreign proteins and enzyme substrates that may be immunogenic, as well as the dependence of light production on the distribution of enzymes, namely extravasation from the vasculature and tissue perfusion. The prevention of dissociation and provision of an anchor for biomolecule conjugation is achieved by encapsulating nanoparticles in polymers, phospholipids, or inorganic shells. QDs’ stable functionalization is still difficult to achieve[29]. The cancer targeting potential of quantum dots has been illustrated in Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The applicability of quantum dots in cancer diagnosis
ce:section.ce:para: QDs can play a key role in the tumour diagnostic process. Due to corrective therapy, phase I problems are typically detected early enough to have a 5-year endurance rate of more than 90%. Technologies that use fluorescence imaging or fluorescence naming are crucial weapons in the clinical resolution arsenal. To enhance fluorescence imaging, quantum dots are majorly used due to their photochemical stability and robust fluorescence emission [30]. QDs have many uses in biomedicine, including studying cell transport mechanisms, functional heterogeneity, membrane transport protein diffusion movements, nonspecific labelling for detection and imaging, contrast of lymph vessels and blood vessels (including micro vessels), in vivo hepatoma treatment, intracellular delivery, internalisation of probes bioassays, Quantum dots by live cells etc. Nanoparticles are known for the endosomal escape and degrade in lysosome, blocking the delivery of anticancer agent. However, QDs, advantageously enhance the transport and penetration of drug under the influence of protease mediated degradation to release the cargo in the tumor microenvironment[31]. In another study, zeta potential was suggested as responsible factor for degradation of QDs-Doxorubicin conjugates, suggesting small zeta potential to be favourable for release of drug [32].

The second-majorly prevalent genitalia women cancer and the main contributor to gynaecological cancer-related fatalities is ovarian cancer. The epithelial antigen carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA 125) is a helpful marker in tumor diagnosis and treatment of ovarian cancer. Although it is crucial for clinical diagnostic applications to be able to see native processes taking place in living things, this ability is difficult to achieve in practise because of the constraints of traditional imaging and the scarcity of appropriate fluorescent markers [33] because of its unique photophysical traits, Quantum dots are prospective fluorophores for in vivo fluorescence imaging which can attenuate a number of limitations of conventional dyes. Several forms of ovarian cancer specimens must be tested for CA125 in order to do so Wang et al. employed Quantum dots with maximal emission wavelength 605 nm (QD605). When QDs and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) were put side by side, it became clear that Quantum dots labelled signals were more focused and brighter, and more stable. In subsequent research, Nathwani developed biocompatible Quantum dots endorsed with the natural protein silk fibroin (SF), and he successfully implemented these QD conjugates as a fluorescence marker for bioimaging ovarian cancerous cells HEYA8. Due to the properties of QDs, there are now more chances for cellular imaging and molecular imaging as well as ultrasensitive ovarian cancer bioassays and treatments [33].

In recent research, L-cysteine conjugated with ZnS quantum dots an electrochemical genosensor was developed to recognize miRNA-200a, an ovarian cancer biomarker. Using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence methods, UV-Vis absorption, the Cysteine loaded ZnS based quantum dot was examined. In order to immobilise the DNA probe, Cys-ZnS-quantum dots are electrodeposited on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode. To enhance the genosensor’s analytical performance, its effective parameters are tuned during preparation. Because the newly produced L-cysteine functionalized ZnS quantum dots offer more active sites, it is easier for NHS/EDC to use the probe as an initiator on the GCE surface. The genosensor’s analytical performance measure miR-200a was assessed using EIS; the findings revealed a concentration in the range of 1.0 1014–1.0 106 M and a detection limit of 8.4fM. A series of one base mismatch can be used to identify miR-200a by the selective genosensor. Also, without sample pre-treatment, the labelled -free genosensor can find miR-200a in human blood plasma [34].

Another research was conducted by Chen found that CQDs/Cu2O composite was more sensitive to SKOV3 cells than to A4549, HeLa, HCT116, HT-29, and normal cells like J774A1, HEK29, and BABL-3T3. The IC50 value of Cu2O/CQDs against SKOV3 cells was nearly three times lesser than that of the other tested cancerous cells and approximately 12-fold lesser than that of the normal cells that was found to be 0.85 μg mL−1. The downregulation of MMP-2/9 and VEGFR2 expression by CQDs/Cu2O in vitro reduced migratory and angiogenesis processes, which may be one of the primary causes of the tumour-specific suppression, because the use of angiogenesis as a tumour treatment method is effective in supplying nutrients and oxygen for the growth and metastasis of tumours. The cytotoxicity assay was performed using MTT on both healthy and cancerous cell lines using CQDs/Cu2O or Cu2O for 24–72 h. The results demonstrated cytotoxicity to be in a concentration dependent manner in all cells, SKOV3 displayed the highest inhibitory rate among all cells tested. Meanwhile at concentration below 12.5 µg mL−1 a discriminative difference was observed between healthy and cancerous cells. Moreover, CQDs/Cu2O showed more potent antiangiogenic effects compared to commercial antiangiogenic inhibitor (SU5416). CQDs/Cu2O also disrupt the F-actin cytoskeleton, which is necessary for many physical cellular processes such as migration, cancer cell death, division, cell adhesion, causing further changes in the cytoskeleton of SKOV3 cells [35] .

HER2/neu or c-erbB-2 also known by the name of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) is overexpressed in about 20–30% of invasive breast neoplasm and is a key factor in the prognosis and choice of treatment for the disease. Wu et al. investigated a technology to label on breast cancer cell membrane by HER2. Following that, a number of researches on the identification of HER2 for the diagnosis of breast cancerous cells with Quantum dots has been performed. In addition to comparing the latest Quantum-based molecular profiling technique with fluorescence in situ hybridization and conventional western blotting, Yezhelyev et al. reported specimens’ interaction with breast cancerous cells to perform simultaneous quantitative analysis of several biomarkers using multicolour QDs (FISH). Using the multicolour bioconjugates, the five clinically significant tumour markers—PR[QD-PR], HER2, EGFR[QD-EGFR], ER (QD-ER), and mTOR (QD-mTOR)—were simultaneously identified in cancer causing cells BT474 and MCF-7. HER2 protein expression and HER2 gene amplification were found to be quantitatively correlated utilising QD-Abs profiling. This discovery raises the scope of employing conjugated quantum dots to identify HER2 protein expression at low levels, although the therapeutic significance of that finding warrants more research. In order to bypass the drawbacks in the clinical applicability of the research mentioned above, a recent study used quantum dots coupled with antibody for analysing of HER2 status in cancer cells of breast. 700 individuals with invasive breast cancer, comprising 3 men and 697 women, were enrolled in the study. In the study QDs immune-histochemistry (QDs-IHC) analytical system was used to automatically, quantitatively, sensitively, and conveniently detect the expression of HER2 in breast cancer. Especially in situations of IHC (2 +), the Quantum dot -based methodology is much sensitive, accurate, and cost-effective than traditional IHC, indicating that this unique methodology may have real-world application potential, especially in impoverished countries [36].

In another research, Naderi and his colleagues examined the impact of variously loaded quantum dots on the treatment of breast cancer. They also discovered that high yield CdS/CdTe and CdTe core Quantum dot caused cell death in cancer cell lines of breast (MDA-MB-468 and MCF-7). The concentrations of QDs have a direct impact on how much apoptosis occurs. The detrimental effects of CdTe, CdS/CdTe and CdTe quantum dots were examined in cell line of breast cancer. QDs can damage cell membranes in culture medium, but internalisation is necessary for them to cause toxicity. QDs can pass through the cell membrane like other biomolecules because of the protein corona present on their surface from absorbing serum proteins. The primary mechanism for CdTe QD internalisation is clathrin-mediated endocytosed. They move in endocytic vesicles that would subsequently combine with lysosomes or early endosomes. More than 50 different acidic enzymes are found in lysosomal vesicles, which speed up QDs destruction and release harmful chemicals into the cytosol. The results showed apoptotic cell of breast cancerous cells [37].

In recent research, Debajyoti et al. synthesised graphene-based quantum dot and graphene loaded with curcumin (Cur) to study their effect on targeting cancer cells death. In phase of cell death percentage, Graphene based quantum dot loaded with Cur and GO-Cur had a synergistic impact on MDA-MB-468 and MCF-7 cells lines of human breast cancer. The cancer cell was subjected to the MTT test in the companionship of GO, graphene-based quantum dots, and Cur, GO-Cur, and Graphene based quantum dot (GQD) loaded with Cur at various concentrations in order to examine the effects of dosage on cell viability, the IC50 value was measured through MTT assay and was found to be 8 + 0.5 ug/mL. Both GO and GQDs were tested in vitro at low concentrations (0–50 g/mL) for up to 48 h, although neither cell showed any toxicity or significant morphological changes. At all concentration levels, the complexes demonstrated a higher killing rate of cells than curcumin alone. Because Cur molecules protect the surface of GQDs and GO, the zeta potential of GO-Cur and GQD loaded with curcumin is higher than that of GO and GQDs. The cytotoxicity assay was performed for GQDs, Cur, GO on breast cancer cells lines at different ratios 1:1,1:3,1:5. Which reported 60% and 80% cell death for 1:1 and 1:3 for cell lines at 100 ug/mL. AFM and SEM images have shown that whereas curcumin molecules on the GO surface cause the bridging of numerous sheets, they produce spherical nanoparticle on the surface of Graphene dots. The delivery of the curcumin medicine into the diseased cell is visible through the optical pictures, and GQD loaded with curcumin outperforms GO-Cur exhibiting better results in both cell lines of breast cancer. Confocal pictures revealed a possible use for the GQDs-Cur and GO-Cur complex in cell imaging. Cur loaded complexes have a limited cell viability towards cells of breast cancer, but can also be employed as a helping tool for cell imaging [38] ( Fig. 1).

Similarly, Zhang and his colleagues synthesised graphene-based quantum-based \nanocomposites for detection of breast cancer cells. In this research, it was demonstrated that using our graphene quantum-based quantum dot nanocomposites, various cellular levels of the tumor biomarker APE1 in healthy cells may be detected quickly, selectively, and with great sensitivity. The normal cell line and the cell line of human cancer (MCF-7) was contrasted. The cytotoxicity was evaluated through cell viability assay, the PANC-1 as living cells showed the survival rate to be 99% and 94% after 12 h and 24 h, whereas for nanocomposite GO the survival rate was found to be 83% and 67% and for nanocomposite rGO to be 82% and 76%, respectively. According to an earlier hypothesis that was amply supported by the observations in living cells, the studied Graphene based quantum dot nanoconjugates act as highly sensitive treatment probes for separating identical types of healthy cells which displayed variable APE1 expression and can be used to separate cancerous cells from healthy cells without altering the protocol [39] ( Fig. 2).

Another research conducted by Wanga and Yan, in which nGO sheets with surface-enhanced CuS QDs were developed to encourage cytotoxicity and apoptosis of breast cancer cells. The modified surface CuSQDs-nGO sheets were thoroughly examined using microscopic and spectroscopic methods. Spectral analysis was used to verify the existence of CuS quantum dots and functionalized oxygenated groups (epoxy, carboxyl, etc.) on the surface of the nGO layer. Additionally, microscopic (TEM and SEM) investigations were used to identify and illustrate the translucent layer with crusty structure. Based on biological studies of apoptosis, cytomorphological changes, and nuclear modifications, the synthesised and surface-modified nanomaterials demonstrated substantial cytotoxic effect against the cancer cells. The CuSQDs-nGO sheets that have had their surfaces modified by NIR laser irradiation have been given increased activity, making them viable cancer therapeutic materials for the diagnosis of breast cancer. More in vivo studies are also desired to create effective breast cancer treatment options [40]. Furthermore, Ghanbariet.al. developed a graphene-based quantum in which the GQDs (GlcN-GQDs) were first loaded with curcumin (Cur), an anticancer drug, and then a targeting agent glucosamine (GlcN), was attached onto the Graphene based quantum dot. With the resulting nanoassembly, MCF-7 breast cancer cells were eliminated. The nano-assembly exhibits sustained drug release behaviour that was pH responsive. As compared to a neutral situation, emissions at an acidic condition were much higher. Given the tumours’ acidic environment, this is ideal. Fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry in vitro cellular uptake tests show that the Graphene dot conjugated Glc nanocarriers is internalised by MCF-7 cells more readily than the non-targeted cells. The drug-free nanocarriers exhibits very minimal cytotoxicity against the MCF-7 cell and normal cells of fibroblast, according to the outcomes of the MTT assay. The increased cytotoxicity for Cur/GlcN-Graphene quantum dot nano-assembly against MCF-7 cells as compared to the Cur/GQDs with less cytotoxicity against normal cells is further confirmation of the probable participation of GlcN as a targeting agent [41] ( Fig. 3).

In a recent invention, Freitas and colleagues successfully synthesized an immunosensor combined with quantum dot for cancer detection. In the study of the biomarker HER2-ECD of breast cancer, a new and effective electrochemical immunosensor with 2-hour assay duration was developed. A detection limit of 2.1 ng/mL, or the 1.18 f mol detection of the analyte (volume of sample 40 L), was achieved using an electroactive QD label to test the analytical performance human spiked serum samples were used, and it generated successful results over a broad linear range with outstanding stability, sensitivity, accuracy and precision. The selectivity and applicability of the recommended method were evaluated and confirmed by analysing different serum proteins in human serum [42].

In one investigation Esgandari and colleagues synthesized a formulation combined sulphur-based quantum dot with anticancer agent for cancer cell targeting in radiotherapy. This study indicated that SQD and 17AAG (at low concentration) improved radiotherapy efficacy in a combinatorial scenario in MCF7 cells. As comparison to monotherapies, radiation therapy combined with 17-AAG and SQD was more successful at slowing the proliferation of cancer cells. According to certain theories, this combination could be used as a successful breast cancer treatment. In conclusion, as 17-AAG and SQD may lessen related toxicity and have therapeutic potential with radiation in BC, they are of major therapeutic value. According to in vitro research, SQD at a lowest lethal dose and 17-AAG at a minimal level may improve the efficacy of radiation therapy without increasing the radiation dose [43].

In addition, Khan and his colleagues synthesised a nanocomposite in which curcumin was loaded into the quantum dot for targeting programmed cancer cell death. Moreover, compared to nonencapsulated curcumin, encapsulated curcumin in the Eudragit demonstrated higher bacterial growth suppression. Samples LN06, LN01, and LN04 were effectively synthesised and their biological characteristics were assessed. TEM and SEM were used to examine the shape, structure and size of LN01, LN04, and LN06. In both MCF-7 and HCT-116, the samples LN06, LN01, and L04 had dose-dependent cytotoxic effects. When the three samples (LN06, LN01, and LN04) were compared, LN04 had a more significant impact on the cells of cancer than LN06 and LN01. Also, the specificity of LN06, LN01, and LN04 was investigated in relation to normal cells (HEH-293), and it was discovered that they had no cytotoxic effects on them. This shows that LN06, LN01, and LN04 only target malignant cells [44].

Contributing to the field of quantum for the purpose of eliminating cancer cells, Zhao and his associates developed a combined therapy approach based on BBPQDs-mediated PTT and PD-L1 immunotherapy. BBPQDs can successfully target localised tumours through tumour homing effects as well as homologous targeting. By immediately killing tumours by a photothermal action, maturing DCs, and activating T cells when exposed to NIR light, the BBPQDs stimulate immune responses for against cancer. The PD-L1 and PTT immunotherapy combination not only successfully treats distant malignancies by reprogramming the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME) and increasing systemic and local anticancer immune responses, but also inhibits tumour dissemination via the immunological memory effect. Also, the BPQDs and BBPQDs’ zeta potentials were investigated. BBPQDs had 23.1–3.1 mV of zeta potential, which is similar to the zeta potential of the membrane of a cancer cell, as opposed to BPQDs, which have a zeta potential of 34.1–4.0 mV. (19.9–3.0 mV). Cytotoxicity results demonstrated that there were no significant cell death even at 180 ug/mL concentration, under NIR radiation cell death efficiency increased and the survival rate was found to be 16.01% thus it was suggested wrapping of cancer cells on BPQDs under NIR condition can enhance the rate of apoptosis. Three significant membrane proteins (gp100, Pan-Cadherin, CD47) were examined through western blotting because they are crucial for immune evasion and homologous targeting of tumour cells. It was demonstrated that BBPQDs have a high photothermal conversion efficiency, which promotes dendritic cell maturation in addition to the immediate photothermal killing of tumours. Findings revealed that Pan-Cadherin, gp100, CD47 and gp100 were substantially enriched in BBPQDs (DCs) Moreover, anti-PD-L1 medication blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 pathways, which enhances immune response of T-cells. T lymphocytes can use it to recognise and destroy malignant cells. By converting immunosuppressive TME into immune-active milieu and boosting both the immune response of systemic and local anticancer immune response, the combination of PD-L1 immunotherapy and BBPQDs-mediated PTT inhibits tumour spread and recurrence. Also, we found that using this combination of therapies protects against tumour recurrence by enhancing immunological memory [45].

The most prevailing form of male cancer in the west is adenocarcinoma of the prostate, which caused 10 million deaths worldwide in year 2020 [46]. Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is used to diagnose this malignancy. The ability of PSA to detect only on-site tumours, whereas prostate tumours have the capacity to spread outside of their original sites, makes it difficult to detect prostate tumours. Additionally, elevated PSA levels may always be false positive, meaning they may be elevated even in men who do not have prostate cancer at all. When PSA and Qds are combined, tumours may be imaged precisely and their distribution, in the case of malignant tumours, can be detected. In vivo imaging of early prostate cancer is made possible by fluorescent probes coupled with PSA [47].

Sensitive and multicolour fluorescent cancer cell imaging in vivo has been accomplished by Gao et al., which showed that for identifying the PSA and androgen receptor (AR) in prostate cancer cells, QDs are more accurate than IHC. These two studies, which highlight the potential of quantum dots as a diagnostic tool, serve as excellent illustrations of why quantum dots are a promising class of nanoparticles. In another research Shi and colleagues showed the potential of Quantum dots as a diagnostic tool for prostate cancer metastasis. Typically, full-length antibodies are coupled to QDs, which results in drastically decreased binding capabilities. In a recent work, it was shown that the usage of antibody fragments of single chain (scFvs) coupled with QDs appears to offer a variety of benefits, including solubility, activity, simplicity in manufacturing, and simplicity in structure-based genetic engineering [48]. By using sole Quantum dots whose trafficking is cancer cell-phenotype-dependent, Rege also created a novel technique for identifying prostate cancer cells with various phenotypes [49].

Jigyasu et.al. evaluated the biological synthesis of CdTe QDs, their characterization and biological activity on PC-3 cells. The outcomes have revealed that CdTe quantum dot induce cell death mediated by induction of ROS generation, cell cycle arrest and apoptosis in human prostate PC-3 carcinoma cell line. 2, 4, 8 and 16 M doses of CdTe QDs were used to treat cancer cells for 24 h. Cell cycle examination and nuclear apoptosis using a flow cytometer, the MTT assay, and an evaluation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) were utilised to examine the anti-proliferative activity against the human prostate cancer cell line. The CdTe quantum dots were roughly 2 nm in size. CdTe QDs caused dose-dependent cell death and nuclear apoptosis, according to an in vitro anti-proliferative study. In PC-3 cells, CdTe QDs significantly increased the quantity of ROS in a dose-dependent manner. Moreover, the G2/M phase of the cell cycle is where CdTe also stopped the proliferation of PC-3 cells [50].

Rajalakshmi and his colleagues in their study used a single-step hydrothermal approach to synthesise a novel TAG-based CD from its precursor TAG for imaging of the prostate cancer. The produced TAG-carbon dots were effectively used for the citrate selective detection. The mechanism of Lewis acid—an electrostatic interaction between the citrate ion and the Lewis acid carbocation in the aromaticity (CN6) guanidinium moiety—is a key component of the sensing mechanistic method. Moreover, the guanidine protons engaged in hydrogen bonding interactions with citrate. According to the linearity curve, 4 nm [S/N = 3] was considered as the detection limit. While binding with citrate the emission intensity of TAG-CDs at 396 nm was quenched linearly. The probe TAG-CDs demonstrated good citrate selectivity, increased interference tolerance, a steady response within 15 min, and good responsiveness under physiological pH circumstances. Finally, TAGCDs advantageously demonstrated cell killing effect and delivered contrast pictures in citrate presence and absence when used for intracellular imaging of citrate in live MCF-7 cells. Citrate levels in human urine samples were identified by TAG-CDs, and the acquired results were verified using the HPLC method [51].

With an average survival span of just under six months, less than 5% of those with pancreatic cancer might survive > 5 years. The majority of patients only acquire diagnoses at later stages that are deprived of specific symptoms and diagnostic limitations, which is a distressing situation. Quantum dots can target the objective of early treatment of pancreatic cancer, at an initial development stage, by using proteins and peptides targeted against overexpressed surface receptors on tissues and cancerous cells, such as the urokinase plasminogen activator receptor (UPAR), transferring receptor, and the antigen claudin-4. CdS/Zns/CdSe quantum dots with greater stability and photoluminescence efficiency as an optical agent was used for cancerous cell imaging of pancreas by transferring and anti-claudin-4. The anti-claudin-4, a monoclonal antibody is also used to demonstrate uptake specific to pancreatic cancer. This tailored QDs technology will be further customised to provide a pancreatic cancer early detection imaging tool [52].

Yong and his colleagues employed non-cadmium-based Quantum dots as efficacious and non-toxic optical probes for visualising cancer cells of pancreas. By further bio conjugating antibodies specific to pancreatic cancer, like anti-claudin 4, to the functionalized InP/Zinc Sulphide quantum dots, pancreatic cancer cell types may be selectively targeted in vitro. The discovery of meagre in vitro targeting in neoplastic cell lines other than pancreas lacking the claudin-4 receptor served as further evidence that the bioconjugates were delivered to the receptors by means of the receptors. The outcome demonstrates a huge potential of InP/Zinc sulphide quantum dots as non-cadmium-based optical imaging nanoprobes for reliable and safe diagnostic imaging [53].

In another research, Zhang and colleagues conducted research and created an exosomes diagnostic biochip that is ultrasensitive and selective for identifying circulating tumour exosomes in serum. In this study, MCF10A epithelial cells of mammary gland of humans, B16F10 melanoma cells of mouse skin, and Panc-1 human pancreatic cancer cells P were all employed. The suggested test used PCs to significantly increase the fluorophore’s emission intensity rather than any enzymes to achieve signal amplification. With the optimised parameters, the built-in platform has an outstanding dynamic detection range between 1.0 109 and 1.0107 particles/mL. Also, the created approach might be used to analyse complicated biological samples in addition to isolated exosomes. Signal amplification is accomplished using an exosomes diagnostic biochip by fusing the benefits of QDs with a photonic crystal of periodic nanostructure that mimics biological processes. Exosomes from pancreatic cancer include the membrane-anchored protein glypican-1 (GPC1), and employing beacons of nano-sized with exceptional efficient luminescence, photonic crystals amplified signals. The technique also made it possible to quantitatively analyse a number of surface proteins on exosomes which are disease-specific. We were able to discriminate between simulated patient’s sample of serum with pancreatic cancer and healthy samples using the DBPCs assay, showing that the technique may hold a lot of promise as a clinical assay [54].

In another research performed by Yang and his colleagues demonstrated that quantum loaded anticancer drug has better targeting action. BCPV/DOX/GQD siRNA nanoconjugates have proven to be capable of delivering the gene therapy siRNA and the anticancer medication DOX simultaneously into cells of MiaPaCa-2. The photothermal effect allows the laser light trigger to selectively release the two payloads at a predetermined time and location. By employing nanoconjugates as the treatment formulation, the expression of KRAS gene MiaPaCa-2 cells was successfully repressed, which subsequently led to a noticeable suppression of the cellular substances such as migration, invasion and proliferation. The cytotoxicity results demonstrated that 90% of cells were viable even at doses upto 4 ug/mL of GQDs and 160 ug/mL at of BCPV, thus it was concluded that both GQDs and BCPV are highly biocompatible and nontoxic even at high doses. It was observed that 1 mg of GQD was able of loading 1.6 μg of DOX. Most specifically, the antitumorigenic activities of the nanoconjugates were improved significantly improved by post exposure to the proper laser light for the siRNA and DOX release. DOX/BCPV/GQD/siRNA nano system were expected to perform well in vivo depending on outcomes of colony formation. The consequences suggest that GQD, DOX, BCPV, and siRNA nanocomplexes will likely be the most effective drug and gene delivery platforms for the diagnosis of pancreatic cancer in the future [55].

Further development in the field of quantum dots led Li and his colleagues found that when QD-RGD injected at intratumoral level at an amount that produces acceptable in vivo tumour imaging causes no long term or short-term damage in mice, demonstrating the therapeutic relevance of these nanocarriers. This research used the S1990 human pancreatic cancer cell line. Given their relative non-toxicity and simplicity of manufacture, RGD-encapsulated Quantum dots can bring forward practical, high-performing fluorescent nanoprobes for a range of bioimaging applications and maybe as a photosensitizer for PDT. Further studies have revealed many methods in which nanoparticles can exist. Intratumoral dose is an effectual way to accumulate nanomaterial in tumours than intravenous injection, as shown by the fact that animals given a comparable intravenous dose (5 pmol) of Quantum dots-RGD did not display the same beneficial results as compared to the intratumoral injection class. Photosensitizers kept in the tumour area during subsequent PDT sessions increases where the lighting is being done. Intratumoral injection is suggested as the best procedure since it uses smaller concentrations of nanomaterials to provide higher fluorescence intensity effects. On the other hand, the outcomes with the unconjugated Quantum dots class were in keeping with the discovery that human pancreatic neoplasm cell lines overexpress integrin v3 receptors, but not the endothelium that already exists in healthy tissues. Integrin serves as a viable target for imaging, antiangiogenic therapy and chemotherapy because tumour cells overexpress integrin 3 receptors [56].

In one research conducted by Ajgaonkar and colleagues created an affordable, biocompatible, nitrogen encapsulated graphene-based quantum dots sensor for pancreas miRNA (miRNA-132) in cancerous lesions. The target gene’s complementary binding to the bait ssDNA on the NGQD surface causes a fluctuation in the intrinsic fluorescence intensity of NGQDs, which serves as the basis for the sensing mechanism. NGQDs are produced from a compatible glucosamine precursor in one-step hydrothermal microwave-assisted procedure, making them biocompatible and water-soluble at concentrations more than 1 mg/mL. The NGQD, which serves as the detecting platform, is cationic (1.14 mV), while bait ssDNA detecting sequence was negatively charged (22.4 6.00 mV). The graphitic surface of the NGQD is electrostatically and coupled with ssDNA. The fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen enclosed Graphene based QDs changed when it attached to the ssDNA sequence, most likely as a result of charge exchange from the bait ssDNA as it created 20 nm complexes. The resulting NGQDs/ssDNA sensor’s specificity and sensitivity were evaluated against miRNA and ssDNA targets. In a dose dependent way, the sensor only reacted to the complimentary target because it had very poor sensitivity in the micromolar range. In comparison to ssDNA sequences that replicated stem single stranded and the loop portions of miRNA, the platform reacted to the real miRNA more forcefully. Both of the sensors for the loop and the stem regions of miRNA-132 displayed dose dependency at the correspondingly low concentrations of 6.85 and 2.74. Its capability of detection justifies the prospect of detection of ex vivo cancer miRNA because it is simple, affordable, and non-invasive [57]. GO and GoQdot were also produced in another study which were effectively obtained and used for miR-141 biosensing applications. By employing SPE to hybridise ss-DNA@GoQdot@miR-141, an effective and successful electrochemical sensor for the label-free detection of miR-141 has been developed. Attention has been made to make sure the developed sensor is ready quickly and offers ideal conditions. A linear range of 6.1–2.3 nm, 0.27 pm of LOQ and 0.091 pm of LOD, are just a few of the results that demonstrate very effective performance metrics. Moreover, miR-21 was used in trials to test the selectivity of the sensor that was created. This led to the development of a rapid, user-friendly, sensitive electrochemical sensor, which when combined with a mobile phone represented a substantial advancement in the early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer [58].

The underlying cause of cancer-related death worldwide and one of the malignancies that are most frequently diagnosed is lung cancer. Conventional chemotherapy, the primary form of diagnosis for small cell lung cancer, lacks selectivity and is therefore related with serious side effects. Therefore, an innovative targeted drug delivery (TDD) method based on quantum dots was thus developed investigating quantum dot. Wu and his colleagues invented a high specific recognition-before-labelling technique for detection of cancerous cells based on quantum dots and aptamers, with a detection of low limit of 6 × 103 cells/mL, in a recent study using single units of fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) and recognition elements as DNA aptamers. Aptamers were used to distinguish cancer cells prior to the labelling of QDs during the cell recognition procedure. As a result, aptamers avoided the effects of steric hindrance while maintaining high selectivity to recognise cancer cells. This prevented QD aggregation. Moreover, QDs displayed robust fluorescence, excellent photostability, and S/B, all of which aided in raising the sensitivity of detection. The identification-before-labelling technique using QD and aptamers thus demonstrated simplicity and sensitivity [59].

In another research HABAB dendron with a thiolate-planting group wrapped around Zns [shell] and CdSe (core)QDs by effective S–Zn bond attachment was created by Chen and his colleagues. Normal lung cell lines IMR90 as well as A4549, H1299 and cancer cell lines of lung CL15 were used in the study. The IC50 value were found to be 1.6 and 3.0uM for the respected cell lines. The resulting nanohybrid, nano-HTPB 14, can be effectively administered inside the lung cancer nuclei cells and is water-soluble. Compared to its parent HTPB, it enhances the acetylation of the amino terminals in proteins of the nonhistone and histone groups and increases the toxicity of cancer cells. The nanohybrid-HTPB 14 causes lung cancer cells to eventually undergo apoptosis by inducing cell phase obstruction during the G2/M stage [60].

Furthermore, Duman and his colleagues conducted research on NIR-emitting Ag2S Quantum dots with a PEI/2MPA coating conjugated with Cet and showed that these nanoparticles were highly efficient at delivering 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) to lung cancer cells that overexpress the EGFR gene. Whereas PEGylation improved the loading of 5-FU onto the polymeric corona and decreased the toxicity and nonspecific uptake of cationic nanoparticles, which are extremely hazardous, respectively. These relatively tiny QDs, which contain 0.874 wt% Cet and 7.34 wt% 5FU, are insufficient as a free medication to treat patients effectively. Due to increased intracellular accumulation and severe induction of death in vitro tests, Cet attachment to PEGylated QDs reduced their biocompatibility, especially in adenocarcinoma human A549 basal epithelial cells of alveolar. When given with mPEG-Ag2S-Cet, the IC50 value of 5-FU was decreased from around 11.5 mg/mL in A549 cells to 5.34 mg/mL, but it never reached cells of HT1299, even at the maximum dose utilised in this investigation (11.5 mg/mL). According to research, optically traceable mPEG-Ag2S-Cet/5-FU kills cells far more effectively than free 5-FU, especially apoptotic cells. Cet conjugation in this amount permits the drug’s targeted delivery to cells that overexpress the EGFR while reducing toxicity [61].

With the motive of combining quantum dot with modern technology Wang and his colleagues created nano sensor of single quantum dot for sensitive detection of human tumour tissues and miRNA-155 in cancer cells that was not dependent on copper or enzymes. This nano sensor can distinguish between a single base mismatch and miRNA-155. Further; this nano sensor can be used to quantify miRNA-155 at the single-cell level in HeLa and MCF-7 cells. Also, this nano sensor can discriminate between the expression standards of miRNA-155 in tissues from patients of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and healthy individuals. Significantly, by simply switching the matching DNA probes, this nano sensor offer a flexible standard for the successful detection of different nucleic acid sequences [62].

In research, Diáz-Garca and his associates studied these processes (cytokines, signalling proteins, etc.) to characterise the initial phases of tissue-specific build-up and dispersion of B16F10 cells in C57BL/6 mice and to assess the effects of therapeutic agents and various biological factors. This research improved our comprehension of these occurrences. The demonstrated that 5 min post injection, the cells began to preferentially cluster in the C57BL/6 mice lungs. So, despite the fact that internalisation of QDs-GSH precludes B16F10 cells from producing lung nodules; the early stages of colonisation of distant organs by Quantum dots-GSH-labelled B16F10 cells can be monitored successfully. The uptake of QDs-GSH’ in B16F10 cells causes a rise in ROS and cell migration, however NAC treatment can counteract these effects. Furthermore, NAC therapy significantly improves the lifespan and fluorescence intensity of QDs-GSH-labelled cells. Because of the cadmium in the quantum dots-GSH core, internalisation of the drug greatly decreased B16F10 cell proliferation and invasiveness as well as the emergence of nodules of metastatic lung. Furthermore, it was shown that Quantum dots-GSH-labelled B16F10 cells can be used for early distribution and can detect the in vivo migration and early of B16F10 cells in C57BL/6 organs [63].

Kulkarni and associates showed how QDs can be used as theragnostic instruments for treating non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC). The experiment’s findings support the idea that QDs can serve as a multipurpose drug delivery strategy for the diagnosis of NSCLC. Similar to this, a cell recovery study shows that compared to pure medicines, a delivery system has a higher potential for internalisation in cells over a specific period of time. Drug delivery methods improve a medication’s ability to enter cells, which results in more drug being delivered intracellularly and having a stronger cytotoxic effect. This fundamental requirement was demonstrated in a cell recovery investigation that showed QD-OSI 420 to have significantly different cytotoxicity from the pure medicines. As a result of their different sizes and surface charges, pure medicines will internalise more slowly than QD formulation. When compared to OSI-420 and Erlo, QD-OSI 420and pure QDs and showed low toxicity when tested on HEK-293 normal cells for possible toxicity towards normal cells. Also, the development of ROS resulting from free radicals generated by cadmium leakage from the Quantum dots core was assessed. The findings showed there was no ROS generation, demonstrating there was no cadmium ion leakage [64] (Fig. 4).

Roshiniet.al. conducted a research and synthesised Tan-ZnO QDs and their anticancer properties were assessed. Ultra-nano Quantum dots, which were about 5–4 nm in size, were evenly dispersed and had a consistent morphology. In metastatic human lung H358 cancer cells, the Tan-ZnO Quantum dot formulation greatly increased cytotoxicity and morphological damage. Moreover, the formulation caused apoptosis and nuclear fragmentation. Moreover, Tan-ZnO Quantum dots treatment of H358 cells caused arrest of G2/M phase cell cycle and reduced cell proliferation higher than untreated control cells. In metastatic lung cancer cells[H358], the essential elements of metastasis (Migration and invasion) were severely suppressed. The results also showed that Tan-ZnO QDs regulate apoptosis by downregulating the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2 in the expression profiles and upregulating the pro-apoptotic protein called Bax. pH sensitive Tan-ZnO Quantum dots are drawn to the acidic microenvironment of the tumour. Additionally, the bright properties of ZnO Quantum dots helps in tracking during chemotherapy [65].

Recently Si QD micellesCKAP4 were developed through research by Huang and associates and may be used to image lung cancer tissues. Human 293 T kidney epithelial cell line and human A459 cell line of lung cancer were employed as target cells to examine the cell targeting capability of Si quantum dots micelles. The Si Quantum dot micelles-CKAP4 particles were spherical and had a mean hydro diameter of about 78.8 nm. The Si QD micellesCKAP4 absorbed UV light between 200 and 500 nm. Using an excitation wavelength of 330 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra revealed significant fluorescence at 640 nm. Si QD micelles-CKAP4 demonstrated effective lung cancer cell and tissue targeting in both in vitro and in vivo settings. The in vivo fluorescence-imaging assay also revealed that the Si Quantum dots micelles-CKAP4 were metabolised and eliminated by the kidney and liver. Cytotoxicity and histopathological studies suggested that the Si Quantum dots micelles-CKAP4 demonstrated non-hazardous potential both in vitro and in vivo. Future plans call for Si Quantum dots micelles-CKAP4 to serve as a surgical navigation fluorescence contrast agent for lung cancer. Si QD proved to specifically targeting imaging agent for cells of lung cancer [66].

Oral cancer is another rising concern for the scientists as it proves to be deadliest at stages with the present treatment options has its own major side effects. Overall, 5-year survival rate is highly less as the disease is diagnosed at later stage. Therefore, a new drug delivery method based on quantum dots was developed. In recent research, Mohapatra and colleagues developed a technique in which nitrogen-based carbon quantum dot has been combined in photo thermal therapy for killing cancer cells. Theranostic carbon hollow spheres that integrate the properties of fluorescence imaging and photothermal therapy have been created. The developed luminous hollow spheres exhibit a respectable 44.6% photothermal conversion efficiency as well as a 14.6% fluorescence quantum yield. When subjected to a 980 NIR laser, these nanospheres which generate heat exhibit a discernible thermal ablation effect on cells of oral cancer, as demonstrated in FaDu cells. Its fluorescence properties can also be used to track the healing process as it happens during therapy. This smartly designed nano system has several advantages that could be further examined as a platform for combinatorial photochemotherapy, including good light-to-heat conversion efficiency, optimal nanodrug good compatibility, surface area, luminous characteristic and particle size. In order to test the cytotoxic potential of hollow spheres, a cell viability experiment using the MTT assay was conducted in FaDu cells and HaCaTFaDu. Treatment with 200 g/mL NCQD-HCS-800 nanospheres boosted the vitality of both HaCaT and FaDu cells by over 85% without the need of laser irradiation, demonstrating the great biocompatibility of the synthetic NCQD-HCS-800 nanospheres[67].

Li and his colleagues developed the novel sulphur-based quantum dot as a nano-photosensitizer for the treatment of oral squamous cell carcinoma, which demonstrated lesser cytotoxicity without the need for light irradiation and was shown to have suitable cell internalisation ability and be self-luminous. In comparison to conventional PS 5-ALA, S-CDs may be a more potent for anticancer nano weapon therapy when exposed to light. The high rate of O2 created by the nanostructure, which in turn triggered acute stress response and Ca2+ influx, was assumed to be the source of the over expression of Bax proteins, caspase-3 as well as the down-regulation of Bcl-2 protein. Data from laser confocal imaging as well as flow cytometry and laser showed that 90% of UM1 cells were capable of ingesting the S-Carbon dots post incubation for 24 h. It was believed that the cationic surface of the S-Carbon dots substantially facilitated cell absorption and established the ground for the initiation of the subsequent therapy [68].

Colon cancer also known adenocarcinoma, which later changes into malignant tumours. Chemotherapy, radiation, and surgery are the primary components of modern colon cancer treatment, but researchers are still looking for better treatments. With the development of nanoparticles (NPs), colon cancer can now be detected and treated using quantum dots. In recent research Lo and his colleagues have developed a multi-strategy gene delivery system based on GQDs for reducing the proliferation of cells of colon cancer using an EGFR targeting antibody and the anticancer medication doxorubicin in this work. In this research, HCT116 cells from human colon cancer were employed. Analyses of the zeta potential, photoluminescence, UV-Vis, FTIR, and XPS data show non-covalent encapsulation of Dox as well as crosslinking of GQDs and PEI. According to in vitro tumour suppression capacity data, both Graphene based quantum dots composites, GIGD and GIED, showed greater suppression of cancer cells. The in vivo investigation also revealed that our GIGED conjugates were less harmful than the Dox group and had equivalent ability for tumour suppression [69] ( Fig. 5).

Following the previous research the same team developed a nanoparticle-based drug delivery method based on high anticancer activity QDs conjugated unsymmetrical bisacridines (UAs). According to CLSM research, Quantum dots-UA hybrids were more majorly absorbed by cells than UAs alone in human lung H460 cells. The three endocytosed pathways, CavME, MP and CME may interact to develop such response. Contrarily, in cells of HCT116, Quantum dots-UAs were only up taken by CME endocytosis pathway, and the deposition of the hybrids and their compounds was notably weaker. These results demonstrated earlier study on the greater cytotoxic activity of QDs-UAs in H460 cells when compared to UAs alone. Furthermore, QD-UA hybrids were substantially more successful at inducing cellular senescence in H460 cells than unsymmetrical bisacridines and their hybrids. It's interesting to note that after being treated with Quantum dots-UAs or UAs, HCT116 cells did not experience cellular senescence. Now it has been established that both malignant cells and normal cells apoptosis and senescence were unaffected by free QDs [70].

In another research, to graft MEO2MAX-OEGMA100-X copolymers from the surface of ZnO Quantum dots, Dine and his colleagues synthesised core/shell ZnO@P(MEO2MAX-OEGMA100-X) NPs through SI-ARGET ATRP. It was discovered that the thermos-responsive behaviour of these core/shell NPs can be regulated by regulating the molar ratio of the OEGMA and MEO2MA monomers. Lower values of the LCST, which represents thermos-responsive behaviour, were found in CM. The LCST of the Nanoparticles in CM decreased by 6–4 °C due to the presence of salts after the Hofmeister series. Core/shell QDs' cytotoxic testing on human colon cancer HT29 cells revealed that at concentrations upto 12.5 g/mL, these useful nanomaterials showed essentially no toxicity. However, a stronger cytotoxicity and a fast reduction in HT29 cancer cell viability were seen when the DOX concentration was increased. This discovery creates new opportunities for the creation of cutting-edge cancer treatments and diagnostics [71].

In another research, carbon dots were prepared for anticancer drug delivery and other bio related applications, utilising EFB as a precursor in a hydrothermal carbonization reaction. Further, carbon dots were PEGylated using an easy one-pot synthesis, allowing for improved luminescence, biocompatibility, and aqueous solution solubility. The blue emission feature of CDs-PEG was shown, along with high fluorescence intensity and a large number of hydrophilic groups appropriate for subsequent functionalization with the anticancer medication doxorubicin. In regard to CaCo-2 colorectal cancer cells, CDs-PEG demonstrated great potential as a Doxorubicin nanocarriers, as shown by effective reduction of CaCo-2 cell proliferation DOX gets released into cells because of pH-response. The strong biocompatibility, aqueous phase stability, and ease of synthesis of CDs-PEG make them potentially useful as a novel class of nonmaterial with favourable fluorescence characteristics for bio-related applications [72].

In recent research, Pilch and his colleagues performed research and developed a combination of novel unsymmetrical bisacridine derivatives and quaternary QDs that showed enhanced cytotoxicity towards cells of cancer H460 while shielding healthy cells from the drug's effects. Increased cellular uptake of Quantum dots-UAs showed a biological response in H460 cancer cells, which was shown as an increase in the quantity and strength of fluorescence signals from lysosomes in contrast to UAs. Though the lysosomes' acidic pH encouraged a higher release of UAs from the conjugated Quantum dots-UAs. The results from a noncellular environment, forms stable conjugates at pH 7.4, but at pH lesser than 5.5(endosomes) the UAs get released from the conjugates and 4.0, are consistent with this (lysosomes). We propose that the greater release from the Quantum dots-UAs conjugates of UAs at acid pH are the cause of the UAs' higher cytotoxicity in H460 tumour cells compared to healthy cells. Because cancerous cells have a lesser pH than normal cells, fewer UAs are generated and the cytotoxic effect is reduced. We made the important discovery that novel unsymmetrical bisacridine compounds combined with quaternary QDs enhanced cytotoxicity against cells of cancer H460 while shielding normal cells from the drug's side effects [73].

Another research was performed by Zadeh and his associates in which the prospective applications of graphene quantum dots-Fe3O4 nanocarriers loaded with Cur and targeted with folic acid were assessed. Folic acid was coupled to the GQDs-Fe3O4 to act as a targeting agent (Graphene based quantum dots- Fe3O4-FA), and curcumin, as a medication of anticancer, was loaded onto the Graphene based quantum dots-Fe3O4-FA. VSM, FTIR and XRD analyses were used to characterise the nanocarrier as it was created. Studying Cur's cumulative release profile revealed that its sustained release mechanism was pH-dependent. The release was significantly higher under acidic conditions than under neutral settings. The acidic environment of tumours is preferred. Using the MTT assay on the cell lines of MG-63 and MCF-7, nanocarriers cell viability was lastly assessed. Because of the higher cytotoxicity, folic acid may be employed to target Cur/Graphene quantum dot-Fe3O4-FA nanocarriers rather than Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 for both cell types. It was also demonstrated that MCF-7 cells were somewhat more cytotoxic than MG-63. Also, the toxicity of the created nanocarriers was investigated using the MTT assay, and it was discovered that the cytotoxicity as compared to normal cells is highly low than that of Graphene dots-Fe3O4-FAand Graphene dots -Fe3O4 [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dot as a platform for drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: A particular method for administering and managing medications is demonstrated by quantum dots. Drugs that are transported by nano-transporters have the potential to have a greater impact at low concentrations, travel farther at lower doses, and have less adverse effects on patients. To transport pharmaceuticals to specific organs, a number of natural particle drug-conjugated nano-carriers containing aptamers, folic acid, antibodies and other modifications were created. It is possible to increase the drug's absorption and bioavailability in the body as well as its effect because QDs have a small molecular size, long blood dissemination, medication loading capacity, bonding property, ensuring freight corruption, numerous ligands presence, controlled drug discharge profiles. By predicting the deterioration of drugs by the gut-related proteins, nanoparticles improve drug therapeutic potential. The employment of quantum dots polypeptide nanogels in delivery of drugs has increased the potential for therapy in the identification, malignant diagnosis and imaging. Medication stacking nano-carriers enhances the penetrability of pharmaceuticals in bio-films and modifies layer transport mechanisms to improve drug absorption in cells. Due to their potential to overcome barriers including lack of sensitivity.drug resistance, and solubility, QDs (nanorobots) are in fact the perfect soldiers for targeted delivery actions [17].

In a recent study, Lipofectamine 2000 was combined with quantum dots and siRNA to examine the efficacy of transfection. In order to establish a connection between the QD signal strength and the level of quality silencing, were mixed 1:1 with the transfection agent. The QDs core could be employed as platform for piling various types of drugs. The versatility of the shell structure allows for the development of drug transporters with extensive physiological properties (for example biodegradability, charge, size etc.). The binding of the hypertensive drug captopril to Quantum dots surface accelerated the therapeutic ability comparable to that of the free medicine and allowed for 96-hour monitoring of QDs-drug biodistribution. Li and his colleagues developed a doxorubicin nanocarrier system based on CQDs. After CQD, a mono-(5-BOC-protected-glutamine-6-deoxy)-cyclodextrin (Carbon dot-Glu—CD) moiety was attached to 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA). Doxorubicin was then added to the 5-ALA-Carbon based quantum dot-Glu—CD system. The method was put to the test on breast MCF-7 cancer cells in vitro, and it was discovered to exhibit significant morphological and cytotoxicity disabilities against cancerous cells [75].

In another study chand et.al. synthesized a combination of ZnO QD with Azoles (antifungal agent) to overcome the MDR of Candida albicans. The synthergistic effect was confirmed by spot assay where cell viabilities of candida strains were all eradicated. In vitro cytotoxic effect activity was tested against Hela cell line and was found to be safe upto 25 ug/mL. the results demonstrated that the combination ZnO QDs with drugs potentiate antimicrobial action [76].

In a recent study Olerile and his colleagues developed an nanoconjugate ZnS QDs loaded with paclitaxel for tumor targeting. The results showed an encapsulation rate of 80%, and drug loading efficacy to be 4.68% with a tumor inhibition rate to be 77.85%. The in-vivo and ex-vivo results demonstrated the successful targeting and detection of H22 tumor. Resultant nanoconjugate can be satisfactorily concluded as parenteral drug delivery cancer theragnostic [77]. Similarly, in an another research doxorubicin was loaded onto PEG which was further covalently bonded to ZnO QDs forming a metal DOX- complex for active targeting of CD44 tumor cells. The ZnO QDs complex reported to demonstrate controlled release of DOX under acidic condition due dissociation of metal drug complex. As a result synergistic antitumor effect was achieved due to incorporation of DOX into ZnO QDs [78]. Fig. 6 represents the mechanism of delivery of quantum dot into the cells.

Aqueous synthesis of quantum dot or semiconductor nanocrystals has been major field of research over a period of three decades. This nanomaterial due to their hydrophilic nature increase the interaction with biological fluids, improving their biocompatibility and enhancing the ability to target specific tissues, therefore to confine and evaluate these properties Gao and his teammates performed research and developed a novel method for the aqueous synthesis of QDs, with increased fluorescent efficiency upto 85% through coating of CdTe core with CdS shell. This was achieved through shelling key procedure which assisted photodecomposition of the caping agent TGA (thiogylcolic acid) leading to the slow release of S2- ions in the aqueous solution of CdTe QDs. Further systematic studies performed by Gao et al. revealed that UV radiation degrades the TGA, forming dithiodiglycolic acid. In alkaline condition dithiodiglycolate underwent complex series of reactions forming thioglyoxylate leading to release of sulphide ions. The results demonstrated that the slow release of sulphide ions lead to the deposition of ions on the surface of CdTe nanocrystals[NCs] thereby increasing the fluorescence quantum yield of the resultant hetero-NCs [79].

Similarly in order to develop water soluble quantum dots using biosynthesis process, pang et.al. synthesized CdSe QDs in living yeast cells. The yeast cells were cultured for 24hrs to reach at stationary phase. Further Na2SeO3 was added and co-incubated for next 24hrs at 30 degrees Celsius followed by addition of CdCl2. The detoxification property of CdCl2 inhibited the metal toxicity and enabled the formation of CdSe QDs inside the cytoplasm of the yeast. The results demonstrated that the size and wavelength of QDs can be improved with increasing the incubation time, furthermore the heavy metal toxicity can be inhibited by altering the redox state or formation of intracellular precipitates of metal compounds [80].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent clinical trial and patents for quantum dots-based therapy
ce:section.ce:para:
Till date variety of patents and clinical trials have been performed to evaluate the efficacy of quantum dots-based therapy. Patents and clinical trials have been demonstrated in
Tables 4 and 5.

The composition of the QDs conjugates as well as the cell types utilised have a substantial impact on the transport of QDs/QD bio conjugates, making potent fundamental probes and research tools when biocompatible QDs are generated. Although several novel procedures have been developed for manufacturing QDs, significant problems still need to be addressed in future. Although these issues might not prevent the development of applications in vitro, they significantly hinder the use of malignant rise imaging in humans. Development of more resilient and toxic-free nanosystem for in vivo theranostic application (therapy and diagnostics) is essential for future in vivo applications and clinical scope. The field is currently developing swiftly, and significant breakthroughs are anticipated in the near future. However, problems like the covering shell's degradation as a result shifting the QDs should be considered. It is also important to take note of the overall collection of the RES, including the lymphatic system, spleen and liver. To reduce toxicity and improve detection performance, various efforts should be made to construct distinctive Quantum dots based on their sizes, valences, composition, sizes and surface coatings.

The kidney can eliminate QDs that are smaller than 5 nm, according to numerous studies. Thus, it is crucial to thoroughly assess the biological safety for in vivo uses in long-term pharmacokinetic and toxicological tests using QD breakdown persistence, immune response and excretion. Because to the toxicity of Zn, Te, Cd, Hg, Se and Pb numerous low-toxicity Quantum dots were developed as substitutes. Doped Quantum dots that emit in the NIR range will be created in the near future. In a recent research, Wang and his teammates developed Ag2S quantum dot with NIR-11 fluorescence, emission ranging from 900 to 1250 nm and 20% high quantum yield. Similarly, Bawendi et.al. developed an InAs based QDs which exhibited remarkable tunable properties in NIR-11% and 30% QY in aqueous solution. These novel QDs exhibited high chemical stability with no heavy metal poisoning because of biomolecular wrapping which exhibits the leakage of heavy metals ions from QDs surface. Thus drawing the attention towards NIR-11 focussed QDs in near future [81]. Before the QDs may be used in any medical process, the toxicity profiles will need to be thoroughly examined and researched. Additional research is required to understand how QDs are removed from living systems.

The use of nanotechnology as a pharmaceutical carrier for treatment of various cancers has been intensively pursued. Chemotherapeutic drugs with higher drug content levels can be delivered to the desired areas using nanocarriers. The newest technical wonders known as QDs have properties that could completely change how cancer is diagnosed and treated. Currently, QDs were employed extensively for in vitro applications, such as the identification of biomarkers of cancer cells in molecular pathology, cell invasion, the emphasis on the tumour microenvironment, and the provision of a novel method for enhancing tumour understanding, classification, diagnosis, heterogeneity and therapy. Yet, the in vivo research still led to challenging identification of the leading and compensating mechanisms of cancer microenvironment and invasion. This review briefly examines QDs' applications, mode of action, cytotoxic experimental research findings, and possible applications in the treatment of tumours. QDs have so far shown to be beneficial, despite the basis of the development of QDs nano-transporters for pharmaceuticals has taken some unexpected turns and that this technology is still in its infancy. Another wave of complete and efficient cancer annihilation is soon anticipated with increased researches into these quantum dots. The versatile effect of Quantum dots in cancer therapy are early tumour diagnosis and bioimaging, gene drug delivery, detection, drug delivery and phototherapy. Moreover, Quantum dots are the ideal drug delivery technology due to their superior low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility and high capacity for cells absorption. Combining biological elements with quantum dots demonstrates successful clinical outcomes. As they are made of heavy metals and could potentially have deleterious effects on people if not removed from the body after treatment. Although, these imaging and therapeutic agents may be costly at first, once all the obstacles have been removed, they will be accessible at a reasonable cost.
